summary: The q4 dataset context provides only aggregate issue counts, with every problem (q1 through q10) showing an identical 100 issues each and no proof-status detail, granularity, or differentiating signals. Because the data is uniform and lacks per-issue content, activity, traction, and solvability cannot be reliably distinguished between problems, and no genuine patterns in open issues can be extracted. Improving data instrumentation is the prerequisite for any meaningful prioritization.
problems: All ten problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 issues, suggesting a placeholder, capped, or improperly populated dataset rather than real activity metrics.
The 'Proof Status' section contains no actual proof states (e.g., proved, partial, failed), making solvability assessment impossible.
The 'Issues Per Problem' header is empty, indicating missing or unlinked per-problem breakdown data.
No issue-level metadata (timestamps, tags, severity, contributors) is available to identify traction or recurring themes.
Uniformity across all problems prevents any data-driven ranking of which problems are closest to resolution.
highlights: A complete and consistent set of all ten problems (q1-q10) is registered in the dataset, providing full coverage to build on.
Each problem has a substantial issue volume (100), indicating active engagement once the data is properly disaggregated.
The reporting structure is in place, so once real proof-status and issue content are populated, automated insight generation will be straightforward.

suggested_todos.title: Fix data pipeline so issue counts reflect real per-problem values instead of a uniform 100
Populate the 'Proof Status' field with actual states (proved/partial/open/failed) for each problem
Attach issue-level metadata (tags, timestamps, severity) to enable pattern and traction analysis
Re-run the dataset insight report once corrected data is available
Define a clear schema and validation check for future q-dataset ingestions
